paragraphs.text: Subject: Googles New Android Developer Verification Policy A Dangerous Step Toward Corporate Control Over Digital Freedom Dear Members of the European Commission, I am writing to raise urgent concern regarding Googles announcement on August 25, 2025, that Android will require all apps to be registered by verified developers in order to be installed by users on certified Android devices. Behind the language of security, this policy represents a profoundly dangerous concentration of power in the hands of a single corporation. By forcing all developers to reveal their identities to Google and requiring Googles approval for all app installations, this policy gives Google absolute control over what users can and cannot install on their own devices. Thats over 70 percent of total mobile users. This is not a minor technical change it is a direct assault on user freedom and digital sovereignty. It means that if Google, a government, or any powerful actor decides a particular app or developer is undesirable, they can simply be erased. Users will have no recourse. Developers will lose the right to create or share software without corporate permission. This is, in effect, a private licensing system for human creativity and digital expression, controlled entirely by Google. The consequences are chilling: > Developers will lose anonymity. Those who create privacy tools, political communication apps, or independent media software could be identified and targeted by hostile regimes. Google can be compelled to reveal their identities, putting lives at risk. > Users lose control of their own devices. Google can silently decide what software people are allowed to install, turning personal smartphones into devices of corporate censorship. > Governments gain leverage through Google. Any authoritarian or repressive regime could pressure Google to block or deanonymize dissidents. This level of centralized control over the flow of software and information is not safe it is control, and it is indistinguishable from censorship by proxy. It is morally wrong for a private company to wield such unchecked power over billions of users and developers. This danger is not hypothetical. In the Epic Games v. Google trial, a U.S. jury found that Google had already engaged in illegal monopolistic behavior by suppressing alternative app stores and coercing developers to maintain its dominance. Now, this new developer verification requirement expands that same monopoly power under the pretext of security, turning the Android ecosystem into a closed, permission-based system. I respectfully urge the European Commission to: Investigate this new Android policy under the Digital Markets Act (DMA) and competition law, as it clearly extends Googles gatekeeping role far beyond what is acceptable; Assess its violation of fundamental rights, including privacy, freedom of expression, and digital autonomy; Ensure that users and developers in the EU retain the ability to install and create software independently of any corporate control ,Act decisively to prevent any company, no matter how large, from holding such unilateral power over the worlds software ecosystem. Googles policy is not simply anti-competitive it is anti-freedom, anti-privacy, and ultimately anti-human. It undermines the open principles on which the internet was built and turns innovation into something that requires permission. The EU has a historic opportunity to defend digital freedom at a global scale. I urge you to act before this precedent normalizes corporate control over what we can create, install, or even think online. Sincerely, Yash
